title: Budgeting
description: A budget is a plan for your money. It tracks what you earn and what you spend.
example: Following the 50/30/20 rule: 50% for needs, 30% for wants, and 20% for savings.
question: Have you tracked your spending over the last 30 days?

title: Saving vs. Investing
description: Saving is parking money for short-term goals. Investing is making your money grow over the long term.
example: Saving for a vacation next year vs. investing for retirement in 30 years.
question: Are you saving cash right now when you should be investing it?

title: Compound Interest
description: Money grows faster when interest is earned on both the principal and the past interest.
example: ₹1000 at 10% grows to ₹1100, then ₹1210 next year. It's interest on your interest!
question: Do you invest regularly to benefit from compounding?

title: Emergency Fund
description: A safety net of cash for unexpected expenses like medical bills or job loss.
example: Having 3 to 6 months' worth of living expenses in a highly liquid savings account.
question: If your car broke down today, could you pay for it without debt?

title: Inflation
description: The rate at which the general level of prices for goods and services rises, eroding purchasing power.
example: A cup of coffee that cost ₹50 ten years ago might cost ₹150 today.
question: Is the interest on your savings account beating inflation?

title: Credit Score Basics
description: A number representing your creditworthiness, based on your borrowing and repayment history.
example: Paying your credit card in full and on time every month boosts your score.
question: Do you know your current credit score?

title: Diversification
description: Spreading your investments around to reduce risk. 'Don't put all your eggs in one basket.'
example: Investing in stocks, bonds, and real estate instead of just one company's stock.
question: Is your current portfolio relying too heavily on one asset?

title: Assets vs. Liabilities
description: An asset puts money in your pocket. A liability takes money out of your pocket.
example: A rental property generating income is an asset. A personal car loan is a liability.
question: Are you buying assets or just collecting liabilities?
